f3307724-f19c-419a-87d6-c8ad79053ee8.question: this query suddenly got slow after last night's maintenance

rows: SELECT o.order_id, o.total, c.name, c.email FROM Orders o JOIN Customers c ON o.customer_id = c.id WHERE o.status = @status AND o.created_date > @date
SELECT p.product_id, p.name, SUM(oi.quantity) as total_sold FROM Products p JOIN OrderItems oi ON p.product_id = oi.product_id GROUP BY p.product_id, p.name
UPDATE CustomerStats SET last_purchase_date = @date, total_orders = total_orders + 1 WHERE customer_id = @cid

step_results.step_description: Plan forcing candidates with remediation commands
rows: UPDATE STATISTICS dbo.Orders IX_Orders_Status_Date WITH FULLSCAN;
Low â€” plan 8420 was stable for 8 months
UPDATE STATISTICS dbo.Products IX_Products_Category WITH FULLSCAN;
Low â€” plan 7201 was stable for 6 months
Medium â€” validate in non-prod first

step_results.step_description: Statistics update history correlated with query regression

checkpoint_log.claude_assessment: Three queries in SalesDB regressed simultaneously starting at 02:14-02:20 on June 21, immediately following the nightly maintenance window. Query 2841 is the primary regression with a catastrophic 100x degradation (48ms → 4,820ms) across 18,420 recent executions. The cascading regression pattern across queries 2841, 1204, and 3102 — all touching Orders/Customers/OrderItems tables — strongly implicates a statistics update on a shared high-cardinality table (likely Orders or Customers) that caused the query optimizer to choose different, inferior execution plans. The precise timestamp alignment with the 02:00 auto-update statistics job makes statistics-driven plan regression the primary hypothesis.
checkpoint_log.rationale: Query 2841 is unambiguously the critical regression requiring immediate investigation. The next logical step is qs_plan_comparison to identify exactly what changed in the execution plan (e.g., index scan vs seek, hash join vs nested loops, parallelism changes) for query 2841. This is essential before considering plan forcing, as we need to confirm the plan change and identify the last known good plan_id to force. Skipping directly to plan forcing without seeing the plan diff would be premature. The stats change timeline (qs_stats_change_timeline) is valuable corroborating evidence but secondary — plan comparison directly reveals the actionable fix.
checkpoint_log.interactive_summary: Query 2841 suffered a 100x performance collapse (48ms → 4,820ms) starting precisely at 02:14 after last night's maintenance, with two related queries also regressing — this is a classic statistics-triggered plan regression in SalesDB, and the immediate next step is to compare the last good vs. current bad execution plan for query 2841 to pinpoint the plan change and prepare a plan-forcing fix.

checkpoint_log.claude_assessment: Root cause is definitively confirmed: a statistics update during last night's maintenance window caused SQL Server to underestimate row count from 8,420 to 1 for query 2841. This triggered a plan change from an efficient Hash Join using covering index IX_Orders_Status_Date (plan 8420, 48ms avg) to an inefficient Nested Loop Join with a clustered index scan (plan 8891, 4820ms avg) — a 100x degradation across 18,420 executions. The cardinality estimate of 1 row vs 8,418 actual is the textbook signature of stale or corrupt statistics causing optimizer plan regression. The fix is unambiguous: force plan 8420 immediately via sp_query_store_force_plan, then investigate and correct the statistics issue to restore accurate estimates long-term.
checkpoint_log.rationale: The plan comparison results provide a complete and unambiguous diagnosis — no additional evidence is needed. Skipping qs_stats_change_timeline because while it would confirm the statistics update timeline, the root cause (statistics-induced cardinality underestimate of 1 vs 8,418 actual rows) is already proven by the plan comparison data, and the maintenance window timing corroborates it. Every second of delay costs performance across 18,420+ executions. The immediate priority is generating the sp_query_store_force_plan command via qs_plan_forcing_candidates to restore plan 8420 and halt the bleeding. Statistics remediation can follow as a secondary action.
checkpoint_log.interactive_summary: Found it: last night's statistics update caused SQL Server to estimate just 1 row instead of 8,418, forcing a switch from a fast Hash Join (48ms) to a slow Nested Loop clustered scan (4,820ms) — running sp_query_store_force_plan to lock in the good plan 8420 immediately will restore performance while you fix the statistics.

checkpoint_log.step_description: Plan forcing candidates with remediation commands
checkpoint_log.claude_assessment: Three queries regressed simultaneously at ~02:14–02:20 on 2026-06-21, correlating precisely with a maintenance window. The root cause is clear: statistics updates during maintenance caused the query optimizer to recompile plans with stale or skewed row estimates. The smoking gun is query 2841: the bad plan (8891) estimated 1 row but processed 8,418, triggering a Nested Loop Join against PK_Orders with 984,200 logical reads versus 1,840 for the good Hash Join plan. All three good plans have been identified and are low-risk to force. The permanent fix requires FULLSCAN statistics rebuilds to correct the histograms that were likely sampled at a low rate during maintenance. Query 3102 carries medium risk and should be validated before forcing.
checkpoint_log.rationale: Plan forcing candidates are confirmed and actionable, but the statistics change timeline step is still valuable before finalizing recommendations. It will confirm whether the maintenance window triggered low-sample-rate statistics updates on these specific objects, which directly explains why the optimizer produced bad row estimates (1 vs. 8,418 actual for query 2841). This evidence will strengthen the permanent-fix recommendation and determine whether the auto-update statistics sampling rate or maintenance job configuration needs to be adjusted to prevent recurrence. The step is quick and adds material diagnostic value.
checkpoint_log.interactive_summary: All three regressed queries have identified good plans ready to force — query 2841 is the critical fix (4,820ms → 48ms) caused by a Nested Loop on a 1-row estimate that was actually 8,418 rows — run the statistics change timeline now to confirm the maintenance job triggered low-sample statistics updates, then force all three plans immediately and schedule FULLSCAN statistics rebuilds during low-traffic hours as the permanent fix.

checkpoint_log.step_description: Statistics update history correlated with query regression
checkpoint_log.claude_assessment: Root cause fully confirmed across all four diagnostic steps. AUTO_UPDATE_STATS triggered at 02:14:08 updated IX_Orders_Status_Date on the 42M-row Orders table using the default 1% sampling rate (420K of 42M rows sampled), despite 8.42M row modifications since the last update. This grossly under-sampled histogram caused the cardinality estimate for query 2841 to collapse from 8,418 to 1, which caused the optimizer to switch from a Hash Join using the covering index IX_Orders_Status_Date (plan 8420, 48ms) to a Nested Loop Join with a full clustered index scan (plan 8891, 4820ms) — a 100x regression affecting 18,420 executions. Two secondary regressions (queries 1204 and 3102) stem from the same maintenance window stats updates on Products and CustomerStats. The causal chain is airtight: stats updated at 02:14:08 → bad plan compiled 14 seconds later at 02:14:22 → regression onset at 02:14:00 (QS bucket boundary). Immediate fix: force plan 8420 for query 2841 (and optionally 7201/5840 for the secondary regressions). Permanent fix: rebuild IX_Orders_Status_Date statistics with FULLSCAN, then configure AUTO_UPDATE_STATS_ASYNC with a scheduled FULLSCAN maintenance job or use sp_autostats to disable auto-update on this table in favor of controlled updates.
checkpoint_log.claude_recommendation: Complete causal chain established with high confidence across all five diagnostic dimensions: (1) regression onset time and magnitude confirmed via qs_regressed_queries, (2) plan change and operator-level root cause confirmed via qs_plan_comparison, (3) force candidate and recovery commands generated via qs_plan_forcing_candidates, (4) statistics update timeline confirms IX_Orders_Status_Date was updated with 1% sampling 14 seconds before the bad plan was compiled via qs_stats_change_timeline. No further diagnostic steps are available or needed. Both immediate remediation (plan forcing) and permanent fix (FULLSCAN statistics rebuild + sampling strategy change) are fully specified.
checkpoint_log.rationale: All available playbook steps have been executed and have produced a complete, unambiguous diagnosis. The evidence chain is deterministic — timestamp correlation between stats update and bad plan compilation, confirmed cardinality collapse from 8418 to 1, and a 100x observed performance degradation leave no diagnostic ambiguity. Switching playbooks is unnecessary; the sqlserver-stale-statistics playbook is a subset of what this playbook already covered. The DBA has everything needed to act: a one-line immediate fix and a two-step permanent fix.
checkpoint_log.interactive_summary: Root cause confirmed: last night's AUTO_UPDATE_STATS sampled only 1% of the 42M-row Orders table (420K rows), producing a catastrophically wrong cardinality estimate of 1 instead of 8,418, which caused a 100x slowdown — run 'EXEC sp_query_store_force_plan @query_id = 2841, @plan_id = 8420' immediately for relief, then 'UPDATE STATISTICS dbo.Orders IX_Orders_Status_Date WITH FULLSCAN' to permanently fix the histogram and consider scheduling FULLSCAN stats maintenance to replace auto-update on large tables.

final_analysis.summary: Three queries in SalesDB regressed simultaneously at 02:14–02:20 on June 21, 2026, immediately following a nightly maintenance window. The root cause is definitively identified: AUTO_UPDATE_STATS sampled only 1% (420K of 42M rows) of the Orders table, collapsing the cardinality estimate for query 2841 from 8,418 rows to 1, causing the optimizer to replace an efficient Hash Join with a covering index (48ms) with a Nested Loop Join performing a full clustered scan (4,820ms) — a 100x degradation. Query 2841 alone has executed 18,420 times under the bad plan, confirming sustained, active user impact. Immediate plan forcing via Query Store and FULLSCAN statistics rebuilds will restore performance.
key_findings: Query 2841 experienced a catastrophic 100x performance degradation (48ms → 4,820ms) across 18,420 executions, with the regression onset at 02:14:00 on June 21 — 14 seconds after AUTO_UPDATE_STATS updated IX_Orders_Status_Date at 02:14:08 using a 1% sample rate on a 42M-row table with 8.42M modifications.

The bad plan (8891) estimated 1 row but encountered 8,418 actual rows, causing the optimizer to choose a Nested Loop Join against PK_Orders (clustered scan) generating 984,200 logical reads versus 1,840 for the good plan (8420), which used a Hash Join with covering index IX_Orders_Status_Date.

Two secondary regressions are confirmed: query 1204 degraded 6.5x (284ms → 1,840ms) and query 3102 degraded 4x (2.1ms → 8.4ms), both correlating with stats updates on Products and CustomerStats during the same maintenance window at 02:18–02:20.

All three good plans (8420, 7201, 5840) have been identified in Query Store as stable candidates for forcing; plans 8420 and 7201 carry low risk (stable for 6–8 months), while plan 5840 for query 3102 carries medium risk and should be validated in non-production before forcing.

The underlying structural risk is the default AUTO_UPDATE_STATS 1% sampling threshold on a high-modification, high-cardinality table (42M rows, 8.42M modifications), which produces grossly inaccurate histograms that the optimizer cannot reliably use for plan selection.
recommended_actions: IMMEDIATE (now): Force plan 8420 for query 2841 — EXEC sp_query_store_force_plan @query_id = 2841, @plan_id = 8420 — then force plan 7201 for query 1204; these are low-risk and will immediately restore ~48ms and ~284ms baselines respectively for the two highest-impact queries.

SHORT-TERM (within 1 hour): Rebuild statistics with FULLSCAN on all affected tables — UPDATE STATISTICS dbo.Orders IX_Orders_Status_Date WITH FULLSCAN; UPDATE STATISTICS dbo.Products IX_Products_Category WITH FULLSCAN; UPDATE STATISTICS dbo.CustomerStats WITH FULLSCAN — then validate query plans revert to optimal before unforcing Query Store plans.

VALIDATION (before forcing query 3102): Test plan 5840 for query 3102 in a non-production environment per the medium-risk rating, then force if confirmed safe: EXEC sp_query_store_force_plan @query_id = 3102, @plan_id = 5840.

PERMANENT FIX (next maintenance window): Disable AUTO_UPDATE_STATS on dbo.Orders or configure a scheduled maintenance job using UPDATE STATISTICS WITH FULLSCAN for all high-cardinality, high-modification tables in SalesDB; consider enabling AUTO_UPDATE_STATS_ASYNC to prevent synchronous recompilation blocking, and document a post-maintenance statistics validation checklist to catch plan regressions before business hours.
final_analysis.severity_rationale: Active user impact is confirmed across 18,420 executions of query 2841 at 100x baseline duration, root cause is identified with high confidence as a statistics-driven plan regression from nightly maintenance, and the degradation is ongoing with no self-remediation path without intervention.

checkpoint_log.claude_assessment: The plan comparison data confirms a textbook statistics-induced plan regression for query_id 2841. checkpoint_log.claude_assessment: Three queries regressed simultaneously at 02:14–02:20 on 2026-06-21, consistent with a maintenance window activity (likely a statistics update or index rebuild that caused SQL Server to recompile plans with stale or skewed row estimates). checkpoint_log.step_description: Statistics update history correlated with query regression
checkpoint_log.claude_assessment: Root cause fully confirmed across all four diagnostic steps. AUTO_UPDATE_STATS triggered at 02:14:08 during maintenance and updated IX_Orders_Status_Date on the 42M-row Orders table using only 1% sampling (420K rows), producing a severely inaccurate histogram. Permanent fix: rebuild all affected statistics with FULLSCAN and configure sp_autostats or a scheduled job with higher sampling rates for large tables to prevent recurrence.
checkpoint_log.claude_recommendation: All five playbook steps executed and root cause is conclusively identified. The stats-change timeline definitively links AUTO_UPDATE_STATS at 02:14:08 (1% sample on 42M-row table with 8.42M modifications) to plan 8891 compiled at 02:14:22 with a cardinality estimate of 1, causing 100x regression. Both the immediate remediation (sp_query_store_force_plan) and permanent fix (UPDATE STATISTICS WITH FULLSCAN + autostats policy change) are fully specified. No additional diagnostic steps are needed or available.
checkpoint_log.rationale: The playbook has reached checkpoint 5 of 5 with no further steps available, and all available evidence converges on a single, high-confidence root cause. The stats timeline step provided the definitive causal link: a 1% auto-sample on a large, heavily-modified table produced an inaccurate histogram, which drove a catastrophic plan change 14 seconds later. The diagnosis is complete, the fix commands are known, and continuing investigation would yield no additional value.

AUTO_UPDATE_STATS triggered at 02:14:08 during maintenance on the 42M-row Orders table with only 1% sampling (420K rows sampled), generating an inaccurate histogram that was baked into the bad plan 8891 compiled just 14 seconds later at 02:14:22.

The modification counter of 8,420,420 on Orders.IX_Orders_Status_Date indicates massive data churn since the prior statistics update, which is the underlying condition that triggered AUTO_UPDATE_STATS and made the 1% default sample wholly inadequate for histogram accuracy.

All three regressed queries have stable pre-regression plans identified in Query Store with forced-plan commands ready: plan 8420 for query 2841 (8 months stable, low risk), plan 7201 for query 1204 (6 months stable, low risk), and plan 5840 for query 3102 (medium risk, validate in non-prod first).

Secondary regressions on queries 1204 (Products, 6.5x) and 3102 (CustomerStats UPDATE, 4x) correlate with matching AUTO_UPDATE_STATS events at 02:18:04 on Products and the same maintenance window, confirming a systemic low-sampling statistics update pattern affecting multiple large tables in SalesDB.
recommended_actions: IMMEDIATE (now): Force plan 8420 for query 2841 by executing 'EXEC sp_query_store_force_plan @query_id = 2841, @plan_id = 8420;' on SQLPROD-02/SalesDB to restore sub-50ms response times and eliminate the clustered scan; similarly force plans 7201 and 7201 for query 1204, and validate plan 5840 for query 3102 in non-prod before forcing in production.

SHORT-TERM (within 1 hour): Rebuild statistics with FULLSCAN on all affected objects: 'UPDATE STATISTICS dbo.Orders IX_Orders_Status_Date WITH FULLSCAN; UPDATE STATISTICS dbo.Orders _WA_Sys_00000003_4AB81AF0 WITH FULLSCAN; UPDATE STATISTICS dbo.Products IX_Products_Category WITH FULLSCAN; UPDATE STATISTICS dbo.CustomerStats WITH FULLSCAN;' — this corrects the bad histograms and provides a safe path to eventually unforcing the Query Store plans.

PREVENTIVE (within working day): Disable or override AUTO_UPDATE_STATS for large tables in SalesDB (particularly Orders at 42M rows) and replace with a scheduled maintenance job using UPDATE STATISTICS WITH FULLSCAN or a higher sampling percentage (minimum 30%) to prevent recurrence of low-sample histogram corruption during future maintenance windows.

MONITORING (ongoing): After plan forcing and stats rebuild, monitor query 2841 execution metrics in Query Store for 24 hours before unforcing plan 8420, and establish baseline alerts for avg_duration thresholds on all three queries to detect future regressions automatically within the INC1234567 change review.
final_analysis.severity_rationale: Active user impact is confirmed with 18,420 executions of query 2841 experiencing a 100x latency degradation (48ms → 4,820ms) since 02:14 on 2026-06-21, with root cause fully identified as a statistics-induced plan regression requiring immediate remediation.

checkpoint_log.claude_assessment: Three queries in SalesDB regressed simultaneously starting at 02:14-02:20 on June 21, immediately following the nightly maintenance window. Query 2841 is the critical regression with a 100x degradation (48ms → 4,820ms) across 18,420 executions — this is severe and actively impacting production. Queries 1204 (6.5x) and 3102 (4x) also regressed within minutes of each other. The tight temporal clustering around the 02:00 auto-update statistics job and the shared SalesDB context strongly implies a statistics update on a high-cardinality shared table (Orders or Customers) caused the optimizer to choose suboptimal plans across all three queries. Query 2841 is the primary target for immediate investigation.
checkpoint_log.rationale: The evidence clearly identifies query 2841 as the primary regression victim. The logical next step is qs_plan_comparison to confirm the optimizer switched to a bad plan (e.g., from an index seek to a scan, or a nested loop to a hash join gone wrong) after statistics were updated. This will provide the before/after plan details needed to either force the good plan via sp_query_store_force_plan or diagnose whether a targeted statistics fix is required. Skipping to plan_forcing_candidates would be premature without confirming what changed in the plan. The stats_change_timeline step can follow if the plan comparison implicates statistics, but the plan diff is the higher-value immediate action.
checkpoint_log.interactive_summary: Query 2841 suffered a catastrophic 100x slowdown (48ms → 4.8 seconds) starting at 02:14 on June 21, right after your nightly maintenance — along with two other SalesDB queries — almost certainly due to a statistics update causing a bad plan choice; recommend pulling the before/after execution plans for query 2841 next to confirm exactly what changed.

checkpoint_log.claude_assessment: The plan comparison data provides definitive confirmation of a statistics-induced plan regression for query_id 2841. Plan 8420 (pre-regression) used a Hash Join with a covering index IX_Orders_Status_Date, correctly estimated 8,420 rows, and ran in 48.2ms with 1,840 logical reads. Plan 8891 (post-regression) switched to a Nested Loop Join with a clustered index scan, catastrophically underestimated rows at 1 (actual: 8,418), and now runs in 4,820ms with 984,200 logical reads — a 100x performance degradation. The cardinality estimate of 1 row vs 8,418 actual is the smoking gun: a statistics update during last night's maintenance skewed the histogram, causing the optimizer to favor Nested Loops (correct for tiny row sets) over Hash Join (correct for thousands of rows). The fix is immediate: force plan 8420 via sp_query_store_force_plan for query_id 2841, plan_id 8420. The qs_stats_change_timeline step could add confirmatory detail but is not required to act — the evidence is complete. The qs_plan_forcing_candidates step will generate the exact sp_query_store_force_plan command needed and should be run next.
checkpoint_log.rationale: Diagnosis is conclusively established with high confidence. The cardinality estimate of 1 vs 8,418 actual rows, combined with the join operator switch from Hash Join to Nested Loop and the 100x performance regression starting immediately after maintenance, leaves no ambiguity. qs_stats_change_timeline would only add confirmatory audit trail detail but does not change the diagnosis or the action required. Skipping it in favor of qs_plan_forcing_candidates gets the DBA to the remediation command (sp_query_store_force_plan) immediately, which is the priority given 18,420 recent executions are all suffering 4.8-second latency.
checkpoint_log.interactive_summary: Root cause confirmed: last night's statistics update corrupted the cardinality estimate for query 2841 from ~8,400 rows down to 1, causing SQL Server to replace a fast Hash Join plan (48ms) with a disastrous Nested Loop clustered scan (4,820ms, 100x slower) — running the plan forcing step now to generate the sp_query_store_force_plan command to immediately restore plan 8420.

checkpoint_log.step_description: Plan forcing candidates with remediation commands
checkpoint_log.claude_assessment: Three queries regressed simultaneously at ~02:14-02:20 on 2026-06-21, coinciding with the maintenance window. The root cause is clear: the optimizer chose drastically inferior plans post-maintenance, most likely triggered by statistics updates during maintenance that skewed row estimates. Query 2841 is the critical case — the bad plan (8891) switched from a Hash Join using a covering index to a Nested Loop Join with a clustered scan, inflating logical reads from 1,840 to 984,200 and duration by 100x. The estimated row count of 1 vs actual 8,418 confirms a severely corrupted/misleading statistics histogram drove the plan choice. All three queries have low-risk, historically stable good plans available for immediate forcing. The qs_stats_change_timeline step would confirm the statistics update timeline but is not needed to act — the evidence is conclusive. Immediate plan forcing should be applied now, followed by FULLSCAN statistics rebuilds during a low-traffic window.
checkpoint_log.claude_recommendation: Root cause is definitively identified: statistics updated during maintenance caused catastrophic row estimate errors (estimated 1 vs actual 8,418 for query 2841), driving plan regressions across all three queries. Immediate remediation commands are available. Running qs_stats_change_timeline would add confirmatory detail but would delay the fix for a production incident with a 100x performance degradation executing 18,420 times. The DBA has everything needed to act: (1) force good plans immediately via sp_query_store_force_plan for all three queries, prioritizing query 2841; (2) run FULLSCAN statistics updates during low-traffic hours to rebuild accurate histograms; (3) review auto-update statistics sampling configuration to prevent recurrence.
checkpoint_log.rationale: All diagnostic steps have converged on a single, high-confidence explanation with actionable remediation commands already in hand. The plan comparison definitively shows the statistics-driven row estimate collapse (1 vs 8,418). Continuing to qs_stats_change_timeline would only add a timestamp to what is already known, while delaying resolution of a critical production regression. Stopping and presenting the full remediation plan is the correct DBA-serving decision.
checkpoint_log.interactive_summary: Found it: maintenance last night triggered statistics updates that corrupted row estimates for query 2841 (estimated 1 row, actual 8,418), causing the optimizer to swap a covering-index Hash Join for a full clustered-scan Nested Loop — run the three sp_query_store_force_plan commands immediately to restore performance (query 2841 should drop from 4,820ms back to 48ms), then schedule FULLSCAN statistics rebuilds on Orders, Products, and CustomerStats during your next low-traffic window to make the fix permanent.

final_analysis.summary: Three queries in SalesDB regressed simultaneously between 02:14–02:20 on 2026-06-21, immediately following the nightly maintenance window, with query 2841 suffering a 100x performance degradation (48ms → 4,820ms) across 18,420 executions. The root cause is a statistics-induced plan regression: updated statistics skewed the optimizer's row estimate for query 2841 to 1 row (actual: 8,418), causing a switch from an efficient Hash Join with a covering index to a Nested Loop Join with a clustered scan, inflating logical reads from 1,840 to 984,200. All three regressed queries have historically stable good plans available for immediate forcing via Query Store. Active user impact is confirmed and immediate remediation is required.
key_findings: Query 2841 degraded 100x (48.2ms → 4,820.4ms) and has executed 18,420 times since regression began at 02:14 on 2026-06-21, confirming sustained active user impact at scale.

The bad plan (8891) for query 2841 catastrophically underestimates cardinality at 1 row vs 8,418 actual — the definitive signature of a corrupted or misleading statistics histogram introduced during the maintenance window statistics update job.

Plan 8891 uses a Nested Loop Join against PK_Orders (clustered scan) generating 984,200 logical reads per execution, versus plan 8420's Hash Join on IX_Orders_Status_Date (covering) with only 1,840 logical reads — a 535x I/O amplification.

All three regressions (query IDs 2841, 1204, 3102) occurred within a 6-minute window (02:14–02:20) in the same database (SalesDB), pointing to a single triggering event — the auto-update statistics job — as the common cause across all affected queries.
Query Store retains plan 8420 (stable for 8 months, low forcing risk) and equivalent good plans for queries 1204 and 3102, enabling immediate, low-risk recovery without code or schema changes.
recommended_actions: IMMEDIATE: Force plan 8420 for query 2841 by executing: EXEC sp_query_store_force_plan @query_id = 2841, @plan_id = 8420; — this is rated low risk and should restore duration to ~48ms baseline, resolving active user impact now.

IMMEDIATE: Force good plans for queries 1204 and 3102 by executing: EXEC sp_query_store_force_plan @query_id = 1204, @plan_id = 7201; and EXEC sp_query_store_force_plan @query_id = 3102, @plan_id = 5840; (validate query 3102 forcing in non-prod first given medium risk rating before applying to production).

WITHIN WORKING DAY: Rebuild statistics with FULLSCAN on affected tables to correct the skewed histograms: UPDATE STATISTICS dbo.Orders IX_Orders_Status_Date WITH FULLSCAN; UPDATE STATISTICS dbo.Products IX_Products_Category WITH FULLSCAN; UPDATE STATISTICS dbo.CustomerStats WITH FULLSCAN; — then unforce plans and validate optimizer choices revert to correct plans organically.

BEFORE NEXT MAINTENANCE WINDOW: Review and adjust the auto-update statistics job configuration for SalesDB — consider using FULLSCAN instead of the default sampling rate for high-cardinality tables (Orders, Customers) to prevent histogram skew, and evaluate adding a post-maintenance plan regression validation check against Query Store baselines.
final_analysis.severity_rationale: Active user impact is confirmed across 18,420 executions of a 100x-degraded query in production SalesDB, the root cause is identified with high confidence as a statistics-induced plan regression, and immediate action is required to stop ongoing degradation.

ef46f3fa-03eb-4209-808f-c6d3a95d5fb1.question: Identify why the database is currently sluggish. What active requests, waits, and blocking session chains are dominating?

step_results.step_description: Active requests, wait types, and blocking chains
rows: SELECT o.* FROM Orders o WHERE ...
SELECT o.* FROM Orders o WHERE ...

step_results.step_description: Blocking session chains and lock wait details

checkpoint_log.step_description: Active requests, wait types, and blocking chains
checkpoint_log.claude_assessment: Session 58 (ETL_USER) is the clear head blocker executing a large INSERT INTO FactSales SELECT statement with CXPACKET waits, indicating a parallel query consuming significant CPU (38,420ms) and logical reads (892,100). Sessions 71, 83, and 94 (APP_USER) are all suspended with LCK_M_S (shared lock) waits, blocked directly by session 58 — confirming session 58 holds exclusive or incompatible locks on the target table(s) that prevent the read queries on Orders from proceeding. Session 102 (REPORT_USR) is independently running with ASYNC_NETWORK_IO, suggesting a slow client consuming a large result set from a reporting view but not contributing to the blocking chain. The blocking pattern is a classic ETL-vs-OLTP contention scenario: a bulk insert running without NOLOCK or READ_COMMITTED_SNAPSHOT isolation is locking rows/pages that application read queries need.
checkpoint_log.rationale: The active_requests data clearly identifies session 58 as the root cause of blocking. Running blocking_chains next will confirm the full lock chain, reveal which specific objects and lock types are involved, and expose whether there are any secondary blockers or deeper wait hierarchies. This is essential before prescribing a remediation (e.g., READ_COMMITTED_SNAPSHOT, ETL scheduling change, NOLOCK hints, or MAXDOP tuning). OS telemetry and app logs are deprioritized since the bottleneck is clearly lock contention, not resource saturation or application-layer issues.
checkpoint_log.interactive_summary: Session 58 (ETL_USER running a FactSales ETL insert) is blocking three APP_USER sessions via shared lock waits — run blocking_chains next to confirm the full lock hierarchy and object-level details before recommending ETL isolation changes or scheduling adjustments.

checkpoint_log.step_description: Blocking session chains and lock wait details
checkpoint_log.claude_assessment: A single-head blocker pattern is confirmed. ETL session 58 (ETL_USER) is executing a large INSERT INTO FactSales SELECT with CXPACKET waits indicating parallelism, and is holding a KEY lock on RID 5:1:182844 in the FactSales table. Three APP_USER sessions (71, 83, 94) are all suspended with LCK_M_S waits, blocked by session 58 on the exact same resource. There is no secondary blocking chain — this is a clean single-blocker scenario. Session 102 (REPORT_USR) is running independently with ASYNC_NETWORK_IO, likely a slow client consuming a large result set from a reporting view, not contributing to blocking. The root cause is contention between a concurrent ETL write and OLTP read workload on the FactSales table under the default READ_COMMITTED locking isolation level.
checkpoint_log.claude_recommendation: Root cause is fully identified with high confidence. A single ETL session (58) holding a KEY lock on FactSales during a bulk INSERT is blocking three OLTP reader sessions. OS telemetry and app logs would not add diagnostic value — the blocking chain and lock resource are unambiguous. Recommended remediation: (1) Enable READ_COMMITTED_SNAPSHOT ISOLATION (RCSI) on the database to allow readers to access row versions without acquiring shared locks, eliminating this class of read-write contention; or (2) reschedule the ETL job outside OLTP business hours. If RCSI is not feasible immediately, consider wrapping the ETL in smaller batched transactions to reduce lock hold time.
checkpoint_log.rationale: Two steps (active_requests + blocking_chains) have produced a complete, self-consistent picture: single blocker, known resource, known workloads, no ambiguity. OS-level telemetry (grafana_os_telemetry) and app logs (splunk_app_logs) are not needed because the slowness is purely lock-contention-driven, not resource-saturation-driven, and no application-layer errors need correlation. Continuing would add latency without changing the diagnosis or recommendation.
checkpoint_log.interactive_summary: Session 58 (ETL_USER) is blocking three APP_USER sessions on the same FactSales row lock — enable READ_COMMITTED_SNAPSHOT ISOLATION to let readers bypass the ETL's write locks, or reschedule the ETL outside business hours to eliminate the contention entirely.

checkpoint_summaries: Session 58 (ETL_USER) is the clear head blocker executing a large INSERT INTO FactSales SELECT statement with CXPACKET waits, indicating a parallel query consuming significant CPU (38,420ms) and logical reads (892,100). Sessions 71, 83, and 94 (APP_USER) are all suspended with LCK_M_S (shared lock) waits, blocked directly by session 58 — confirming session 58 holds exclusive or incompatible locks on the target table(s) that prevent the read queries on Orders from proceeding. Session 102 (REPORT_USR) is independently running with ASYNC_NETWORK_IO, suggesting a slow client consuming a large result set from a reporting view but not contributing to the blocking chain. The blocking pattern is a classic ETL-vs-OLTP contention scenario: a bulk insert running without NOLOCK or READ_COMMITTED_SNAPSHOT isolation is locking rows/pages that application read queries need.

A single-head blocker pattern is confirmed. ETL session 58 (ETL_USER) is executing a large INSERT INTO FactSales SELECT with CXPACKET waits indicating parallelism, and is holding a KEY lock on RID 5:1:182844 in the FactSales table. Three APP_USER sessions (71, 83, 94) are all suspended with LCK_M_S waits, blocked by session 58 on the exact same resource. There is no secondary blocking chain — this is a clean single-blocker scenario. Session 102 (REPORT_USR) is running independently with ASYNC_NETWORK_IO, likely a slow client consuming a large result set from a reporting view, not contributing to blocking. The root cause is contention between a concurrent ETL write and OLTP read workload on the FactSales table under the default READ_COMMITTED locking isolation level.

final_analysis.summary: Session 58 (ETL_USER) is executing a large parallel INSERT INTO FactSales SELECT statement and is holding exclusive KEY locks that are blocking three active APP_USER read sessions (71, 83, 94) with LCK_M_S waits of approximately 2.1 seconds each, causing direct user-facing query suspension. The root cause is classic ETL-vs-OLTP lock contention on the FactSales table under the default READ_COMMITTED locking isolation level. Session 102 (REPORT_USR) is running independently with ASYNC_NETWORK_IO waits and is not contributing to the blocking chain. Immediate action is required to relieve the blocked application sessions.
key_findings: Session 58 (ETL_USER) is the sole head blocker executing INSERT INTO FactSales SELECT with CXPACKET waits, consuming 38,420ms CPU and 892,100 logical reads, indicating a large parallel ETL operation.

Sessions 71, 83, and 94 (APP_USER) are all suspended with LCK_M_S waits of ~2.1 seconds, each blocked by session 58 on the same KEY lock resource (RID: 5:1:182844 in FactSales), confirming active user-facing query failures.

The blocking pattern is a single-head blocker chain — session 58 holds an incompatible lock preventing three concurrent OLTP read queries on Orders/FactSales from proceeding under the default READ_COMMITTED locking isolation.

Session 102 (REPORT_USR) is independently running with ASYNC_NETWORK_IO wait (320ms) consuming 284,000 logical reads against a reporting view, indicating a slow client consuming a large result set but contributing no blocking.

The CXPACKET waits on session 58 indicate the ETL INSERT is executing with parallelism, which prolongs the duration of lock ownership and increases the blast radius of the contention on application sessions.
recommended_actions: IMMEDIATE: Evaluate killing session 58 (ETL_USER) to unblock sessions 71, 83, and 94 if business impact of delaying the ETL is acceptable; confirm with application owners before terminating.

SHORT-TERM: Enable READ_COMMITTED_SNAPSHOT ISOLATION (RCSI) on the affected database to allow readers to access row versions instead of waiting on write locks, eliminating this class of ETL-vs-OLTP contention without application code changes.

SHORT-TERM: Schedule the ETL INSERT INTO FactSales job during off-peak hours to avoid concurrent execution with OLTP application traffic, and consider adding MAXDOP hints or a Resource Governor workload group to limit ETL parallelism and reduce lock duration.

MEDIUM-TERM: Review the ETL pattern for batching opportunities (e.g., chunked inserts with smaller transactions) to reduce lock hold time, and investigate whether the reporting session (102) requires result set pagination or server-side cursor to address ASYNC_NETWORK_IO pressure.
final_analysis.severity_rationale: Active user impact is confirmed — three APP_USER sessions are suspended and unable to complete read queries due to blocking by the ETL session, with root cause identified with high confidence as ETL-vs-OLTP lock contention on FactSales under READ_COMMITTED locking isolation.
